1_answer_correctness.justification: Scoring is a deterministic continuous metric (mean ΔNLL/step comparing LLM orders vs Sterman human-calibrated baseline vs leave-one-human-out baseline) computed in nll_gaussian/compute_delta_nll. There is no exact ground-truth answer per item; the 'better-than-LOHO' baseline is reasonable but the metric's sign/scale is not anchored to clear pass/fail thresholds, and parse_order_from_response regex-extracts the first integer with a brittle WAIT-of-default fallback, so verification of the model's intended order is fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: The headline eval genuinely sends per-round prompts to a model (kbench.chats.new + llm.prompt inside run_beer_game_with_llm) and scores the model's actual order decisions against human-likeness, so it measures a model. However, it claims to probe 'executive control / inhibiting optimization' yet the metric only measures how closely the LLM mimics human Sterman ordering behavior (ΔNLL vs human baseline); higher human-likeness is conflated with the claimed inhibitory-control capability, and order-parsing/format compliance is a visible confound, giving partial but imperfect alignment.

3_code_quality.justification: Code is well-organized into clearly sectioned modules (simulator, scoring, Player class, prompt builder, runner, kbench task), uses @kbench.task and evaluate-style structure, fresh chat context per role/week via kbench.chats.new, and includes a robust eta-based pipeline simulation; parsing has a fallback. Two near-duplicate notebook variants (baseline vs enhanced-prompt) are clean and idiomatic.

4_sample_size.justification: NUM_SESSIONS=11 sessions, each producing ΔNLL across 4 roles = 44 scored data points per task, with 24-round trajectories each; well above the >20 threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: A single principled performance dimension is computed (ΔNLL/step human-likeness) with markdown/docstring justification, plus assertion checks for order count and non-negativity. It does not compute a second distinct dimension (e.g., cost efficiency vs human-likeness, or bullwhip/variance amplification), so it stays at one underlying dimension.

6_problem_statement.justification: The notebook docstrings name the capability (baseline executive control in the Beer Distribution Game, inhibiting over-optimization) and describe the ΔNLL-vs-human design, but there is no separate task-page markdown connecting it to a broader research gap or explaining why this beats existing supply-chain LLM evals.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Extensive in-code section headers and docstrings document the simulator, Sterman parameters, scoring formula, and runner. However dataset provenance for _STERMAN_11 (the 11 calibrated human sessions) is only asserted, not cited, and there are no markdown narrative cells explaining metric interpretation/edge cases for a reader, leaving partial documentation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a named domain (supply-chain decision-making / inhibitory control via the Beer Game) and includes two conditions (baseline prompt vs enhanced decision-guidelines prompt) that act as a manipulation. But there is no congruent/incongruent control isolating inhibition specifically; performance reflects general human-mimicry plus prompt-following, so a specific sub-capability is not cleanly isolated.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary naturally across 11 distinct human-parameter sessions and 4 supply-chain roles, and results are printed per role and per session, giving some natural variation. But there is no structured difficulty taxonomy or labeled sub-condition stratification (e.g., demand-shock severity tiers) reported as a designed gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook runs a single injected kbench.llm via .run(kbench.llm); no model identifiers and no multiple-model comparison appear in outputs or metadata, so coverage is effectively one model.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each sub-task has unambiguous deterministic ground truth: the 3D embodied go/no-go passes only if GO is issued after DISTANCE reaches 0 (env tracks failure_reason and total_reward>1.1), the physics task uses MCQ with a known correct letter, and the vision task uses an exact integer goal-count derived from the config name. Verification is exact-match / numeric with robust _parse_action and _parse_letter helpers.

2_construct_validity.justification: The headline eval drives a real model through the AnimalAI Unity environment (llm.respond(schema=Response) each turn, GoNoGoScaffold stepping the simulator) and scores the model's actual embodied actions, so it measures a model. Construct validity is strong: every task ships paired control vs test conditions (e.g. reward-present test vs reward-absent control; goal-count control with 0 goals), and auxiliary physics/vision screening tasks isolate whether failures stem from perception vs inhibition, deliberately controlling confounds for the claimed inhibitory-control construct.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, well-commented across many notebooks: dataclass Response schema, EnvironmentScaffold/KaggleWrapper subclassing, structured-output parsing that raises on invalid actions, fresh chats.new per trial, .evaluate() over scenario/yml lists, deterministic per-trial MCQ shuffling, and video logging. Robust env teardown in try/finally.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset provides 297 config/frame trials; vision-test runs ~143 trials (score 0.993), physics-test ~45 positive-ramp trials (0.911). The embodied 3D test notebook filters to 5 ramp configs but the text-only variant runs 5 scenarios x 10 repeats = 50, and the overall task suite far exceeds 20 scored items.

5_metric_design.justification: Each notebook computes a single pass-rate / accuracy metric appropriate to its task. The benchmark spans multiple complementary metrics across notebooks (embodied success rate, physics MCQ accuracy, vision count accuracy) and uses control vs test conditions, but within the headline eval only one dimension (fraction passed) is reported, and MCQ position bias is mitigated though calibration/second-dimension metrics are not added.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook markdown clearly names the capability (embodied inhibitory control via an Animal-AI go/no-go: withhold GO while DISTANCE>0) and states pass/fail rules, but there is no dedicated task-page section motivating why embodied inhibition matters or what gap it fills relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Documentation is comprehensive: markdown cells explain the environment rules, distance bucketing, action semantics, and pass/fail criteria; the config naming schema (ramp_N_main_X_ramp_Y_wall_Z) is parsed and documented; dataset path/provenance, frame-skip handling, and the control-vs-test filtering logic are all described in code and prose across notebooks.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (prepotent-response inhibition: suppress GO until the reward settles) with explicit experimental controls — paired test (reward will roll into gutter) vs control (no reward) conditions, plus physics/vision screening tasks that attribute failures to inhibition rather than perception. Clear hypothesis embedded in the pass condition.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized by a structured taxonomy encoded in config names (ramp count/slope, main/ramp/wall goal type) and explicitly partitioned into control vs test sub-conditions with per-condition filtering; the embodied test varies starting distance across ramp_2..ramp_10 producing a difficulty gradient, and results are reported per scenario.

10_model_coverage.justification: Outputs show two distinct models across the suite: gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview (text-only task) and gemini-3.1-pro-preview (physics, vision, embodied tasks), giving 2 models — limited cross-model coverage.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is fully specified per test input in the dataset YAML (each test maps input string to its correct output mapping), and evaluate_reply does exact-string comparison reply.answers[k] == v over all tests. Answers are unambiguous and deterministically verifiable; the YAML parser (ModelReply.from_yaml) extracts the fenced block and type-checks fields.

2_construct_validity.justification: The eval genuinely sends prompts to a model (llm.prompt for the intro and for each of 130 sequential tasks) and scores the model's actual predicted mappings against held ground truth (run printed 487 test inputs, 66 correct, score 0.108). It is tightly aligned with the stated construct: learning letter-substitution rules from observations, then carrying/overriding them across sequential tasks tests in-context rule induction, verification, and adaptation — the override-across-tasks design directly operationalizes the 'adapt when new observations contradict' claim.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean dataclass-based design (LearningTask, ModelReply) with to_yaml/from_yaml serialization, type validation, a fenced-yaml extraction fallback, try/except around per-task parsing that records unparsable replies and substitutes an empty answer set rather than crashing, and idiomatic @kbench.task usage.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset contains 130 tasks and the run reports 487 total scored test inputs — far exceeding the >20 threshold and enabling fine-grained measurement.

5_metric_design.justification: Primary metric is a single dimension: fraction of correct test outputs, modified by a penalties_factor that deducts 10% for an unwanted intro response and 10% for any unparsable reply. The penalty terms add format-compliance signal but conflate it with accuracy; there is no second distinct performance dimension (e.g., separate adaptation-vs-baseline accuracy or calibration).

6_problem_statement.justification: benchmark_task_description names the capability (reason out rules from observations, verify, adapt when contradicted, repeat across interactions) and the intro_prompt gives a worked example, which is clear, but there is no broader motivation, related-work gap, or task-page narrative explaining why this matters relative to existing in-context-learning benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The notebook documents the task format, YAML schema, and scoring via the description string, intro_prompt example, and readable code. However there are no narrative markdown cells; dataset provenance/construction of letters_1to1_with_overrides.yaml and the precise override semantics across tasks are left to be inferred from data, so documentation is partial.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability — incremental in-context rule induction with override/adaptation across a sequence — with a controlled manipulation built into the data: later tasks deliberately introduce mappings that override earlier ones, isolating whether the model can update beliefs rather than just memorize. The hypothesis (adapt when new observations contradict previous ones) is explicit and operationalized.

9_item_diversity.justification: Tasks vary in complexity (number/length of observation pairs and test strings increase, multi-letter chains, override cases), giving natural difficulty variation across the 130 tasks. But the scoring only reports a single aggregate accuracy with no per-difficulty-tier or per-sub-condition (e.g., override vs non-override) breakdown, so the gradient is not labeled or reported.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only one .run(kbench.llm) invocation against a single injected model; no model identifier or multiple-model comparison appears, so coverage is one model.

1_answer_correctness.justification: The folder is completely empty — no notebook, dataset, or source note. No ground truth or verification logic exists to evaluate.

2_construct_validity.justification: No notebook or task page exists, so there is no primary evaluation that sends prompts to a model or scores any model output — the strongest construct-validity failure (no model is measured at all).

3_code_quality.justification: No code or notebook exists for the task; per the rubric this is an automatic Level 0.

4_sample_size.justification: No evaluation items exist (0 items), far below the 10-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: No metric is computed because no code exists.

6_problem_statement.justification: No task page or any description is present; a reader cannot determine what is measured or why.

7_technical_documentation.justification: No documentation of any kind (no markdown, no dataset description, no methodology) is provided.

8_capability_targeting.justification: No materials exist, so no capability is probed and nothing about a model could be revealed.

9_item_diversity.justification: There are no items at all, so no diversity or sub-condition structure exists.

10_model_coverage.justification: No notebook and no run; the task has been run on zero models.
